items.q: Does a bigger coefficient in an equation mean a bigger mass of that substance?
items.correct: No — coefficients give mole ratios; to compare masses you must multiply moles by each substance's Mr.
items.misconception: 2H₂ must weigh more than O₂ in 2H₂ + O₂ because its number is bigger.
items.random_wrong: Coefficients show the temperature each substance must reach.
items.why: Students read equations like recipes by count alone; two moles of light H₂ (4 g) weigh far less than one mole of O₂ (32 g).

items.q: Is mass lost when a reaction gives off a gas?
items.correct: No — mass is conserved; the escaping gas carries its mass away, so all products together still equal the reactants.
items.misconception: Mass is destroyed in fizzing reactions because the flask ends up lighter.
items.random_wrong: Mass always increases during a reaction because energy is added.
items.why: Open-flask observations make mass appear to vanish; weighing in a sealed container shows conservation of mass holds.

items.q: Do equal masses of two different gases contain equal numbers of molecules?
items.correct: No — equal volumes at the same temperature and pressure contain equal numbers; equal masses only match if the Mr values are the same.
items.misconception: One gram of any gas always contains the same number of molecules.
items.random_wrong: Heavier gases always contain more molecules per gram.
items.why: Students garble Avogadro's law, swapping 'equal volumes' for 'equal masses'; molecule count per gram depends on molar mass.

items.q: The relative formula mass of CO₂ (C = 12, O = 16) is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 44. “28” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many moles are in 9.0 g of water (Mr = 18)?
items.why: Correct answer: 0.50 mol. “2.0 mol” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, how many moles of O₂ react with 4 mol of H₂?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 2 mol. “4 mol” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The volume of 0.50 mol of any gas at room temperature and pressure is:
items.why: Correct answer: 12 dm³. “24 dm³” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What mass of water forms when 4.0 g of hydrogen burns completely? (2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O; Mr: H₂ = 2, H₂O = 18)
items.why: Exact answer: 36 g. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
